meta.title: AI Fundamentals – Microsoft Learn Practice Assessment
meta.subject: AI-900

questions.question_text: Which type of machine learning algorithm groups observations based on the similarities of features?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Classification assigns data to predefined labeled categories — it requires labeled training data.

b.explanation: Correct. Clustering is an unsupervised algorithm that groups observations by feature similarity, with no predefined labels required.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Regression predicts a continuous numeric value, not groups.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Supervised learning is a category that includes classification and regression; it does not specifically describe grouping by similarity.

questions.question_text: Which type of machine learning algorithm finds the optimal way to split a dataset into groups without relying on training and validating label predictions?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Classification requires labeled training data and predicts predefined categories.

b.explanation: Correct. Clustering is unsupervised — it finds natural groupings in data without using labels.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Regression predicts numeric output values from labeled training data.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Supervised learning requires labels for both training and validation.

questions.question_text: A company deploys an online marketing campaign to social media platforms. The company wants to use machine learning to measure the sentiment of users on Twitter who made posts in response to the campaign. Which type of machine learning is this?

a.explanation: Correct. Sentiment analysis classifies text into categories (positive, negative, neutral) — this is a classification task.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Clustering groups unlabeled data by similarity. Sentiment analysis uses predefined sentiment categories.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Data transformation prepares data for modeling but is not itself a machine learning algorithm type.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Regression predicts continuous numeric values. Sentiment categories are discrete labels, not numbers.

questions.question_text: A healthcare organization has a dataset consisting of bone fracture scans that are categorized by using predefined fracture types. The organization wants to use machine learning to detect the different types of bone fractures for new scans. Which type of machine learning is this?

a.explanation: Correct. The model learns from labeled scans with predefined fracture types and predicts the category for new scans — this is classification.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Clustering discovers unknown groupings; here the fracture types are predefined, so labeled training is used.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Featurization is the process of engineering input features, not a machine learning algorithm type.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Regression predicts continuous values. Fracture types are discrete categories.

questions.question_text: A retailer wants to group together online shoppers that have similar attributes to enable targeted marketing campaigns for new product launches. Which type of machine learning is this?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Classification assigns to predefined categories. The retailer has no predefined shopper groups — they want to discover them.

b.explanation: Correct. Clustering groups observations by similarity without predefined labels, which is exactly what the retailer needs.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Multiclass classification predicts one of several predefined categories. The shopper segments are not predefined here.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Regression predicts a numeric value, not groups of customers.

questions.question_text: Predicting rainfall for a specific geographical location is an example of which type of machine learning?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Classification predicts discrete categories, not continuous values like rainfall amounts.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Clustering groups unlabeled observations — it does not predict numeric outcomes.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Featurization is a data preparation step, not a learning algorithm type.

d.explanation: Correct. Rainfall is a continuous numeric value. Regression models predict numeric outputs from input features.

questions.question_text: Which assumption of the multiple linear regression model should be satisfied to avoid misleading predictions?

a.text: Features are dependent on each other.
a.explanation: Incorrect. If features are correlated (multicollinearity), coefficient estimates become unreliable and predictions are misleading.

b.text: Features are independent of each other.
b.explanation: Correct. Multiple linear regression assumes no multicollinearity — features should be independent of each other to produce reliable predictions.

c.text: Labels are dependent on each other.
c.explanation: Incorrect. Label independence is not a core assumption of linear regression; feature independence is.

d.text: Labels are independent of each other.
d.explanation: Incorrect. While labels should not be perfectly correlated, this is not the primary assumption that prevents misleading predictions in multiple linear regression.

questions.question_text: In a regression machine learning algorithm, what are the characteristics of features and labels in a training dataset?

a.explanation: Correct. A training dataset contains both known feature values (inputs) and known label values (outputs) so the model can learn the relationship between them.

b.text: known feature values and unknown label values
b.explanation: Incorrect. Unknown labels describe new data where predictions are needed — not training data.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Training data must have known values for both features and labels.

d.text: unknown feature values and known label values
d.explanation: Incorrect. Features must be known (measured) to train the model.

questions.question_text: In a regression machine learning algorithm, what are the characteristics of features and labels in a validation dataset?

a.explanation: Correct. A validation dataset has both known features and known labels so the model's predictions can be compared against the true labels to evaluate accuracy.

b.text: known feature values and unknown label values
b.explanation: Incorrect. If labels were unknown, the model could not be evaluated against ground truth.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Validation requires known values for both.

d.text: unknown feature values and known label values
d.explanation: Incorrect. Features must be known to generate a prediction to compare against the known label.

questions.question_text: A company wants to predict household water use based on the number of people in a house, the weather temperature, and the time of year. In terms of data labels and features, what is the label in this use case?

a.text: number of people in the house
a.explanation: Incorrect. The number of people is an input feature used to make the prediction.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Time of year is an input feature.

c.explanation: Correct. The label is the value the model is trained to predict. Here, water use is the output to be predicted from the input features.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Weather temperature is an input feature, not the prediction target.

questions.question_text: What should you do after preparing a dataset and before training the machine learning model?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Cleaning missing data is part of dataset preparation, which happens before this step.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Normalization is part of data preparation, not the step immediately before training.

c.text: split data into training and validation datasets
c.explanation: Correct. After preparing the data, you split it so you have a training set to fit the model and a separate validation set to evaluate it.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Data summarization (exploratory analysis) is done during preparation, not immediately before training.

questions.question_text: You need to create an automated machine learning (automated ML) model. Which resource should you create first in Azure Machine Learning studio?

a.explanation: Incorrect. A dataset is created inside a workspace. The workspace must exist first.

b.explanation: Correct. An Azure Machine Learning workspace is the top-level resource. All other assets — datasets, experiments, models — live inside it.

c.explanation: Incorrect. An Azure container instance is a compute target used for deployment, not the first resource to create for ML.

d.explanation: Incorrect. AKS is a production deployment compute target, not the starting point for automated ML.

questions.question_text: You need to use Azure Machine Learning to train a regression model. What should you create in Machine Learning studio?

a.explanation: Correct. In Azure Machine Learning studio, training runs are submitted as jobs (experiments). A job encapsulates the training script, compute, and environment.

b.explanation: Incorrect. A workspace already needs to exist before you can train a model. Creating the workspace is a prerequisite, not the training step.

c.explanation: Incorrect. An Azure container instance is used to deploy a trained model as an endpoint, not to train one.

d.explanation: Incorrect. AKS is for production model deployment, not for training.

questions.question_text: You need to use the Azure Machine Learning designer to train a machine learning model. What should you do first in the Machine Learning designer?

a.explanation: Incorrect. You add a dataset to the canvas after creating a pipeline. A pipeline must exist first.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Training modules are added to a pipeline after the pipeline is created.

c.explanation: Correct. In the Azure ML designer, the pipeline is the canvas where you build your workflow. Creating it is the first step before adding datasets or modules.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Deployment happens after training, not before it.

questions.question_text: You train a regression model by using automated machine learning in Azure Machine Learning studio and review the best model summary. You need to publish the model for others to use from the internet. What should you do next?

a.explanation: Incorrect. A compute cluster is used for training, which has already been completed.

b.text: Deploy the model to an endpoint.
b.explanation: Correct. To make a trained model accessible over the internet, you deploy it to a real-time inference endpoint, which exposes a REST API.

c.text: Split the data into training and validation datasets.
c.explanation: Incorrect. Data splitting happens before training, not after.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Testing the service happens after deployment, not as the next step immediately after training.

questions.question_text: Which three supervised machine learning models can you train by using automated machine learning (automated ML) in the Azure Machine Learning studio? Each correct answer presents a complete solution.

a.explanation: Correct. Automated ML supports classification tasks, predicting which category a data point belongs to.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Clustering is unsupervised learning. Automated ML in Azure ML studio supports supervised tasks only: classification, regression, and time-series forecasting.

c.explanation: Incorrect. An inference pipeline is created after training for deployment — it is not a training task type.

d.explanation: Correct. Automated ML supports regression tasks, predicting continuous numeric values.

e.explanation: Correct. Automated ML supports time-series forecasting as a specialized form of regression for sequential temporal data.

questions.question_text: What is an unsupervised machine learning algorithm module for training models in the Azure Machine Learning designer?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Classification is supervised learning — it requires labeled training data.

b.explanation: Correct. K-Means Clustering is an unsupervised algorithm that groups data into k clusters based on feature similarity, without requiring labels.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Linear Regression is a supervised algorithm that requires labeled (numeric) training data.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Normalize Data is a data transformation module, not a machine learning training algorithm.

questions.question_text: Which natural language processing (NLP) technique assigns numeric values to words such as 'plant' and 'flower' so that they are considered closer to each other than a word such as 'airplane'?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Frequency analysis counts how often words appear — it does not encode semantic similarity between words.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Lemmatization reduces words to their root form (e.g., 'running' → 'run') but does not capture semantic relationships between different words.

c.explanation: Incorrect. N-grams model word sequences (e.g., pairs or triples of adjacent words) but do not encode semantic similarity.

d.explanation: Correct. Vectorization (word embeddings) encodes words as numeric vectors in a semantic space where semantically similar words have vectors that are closer together.

questions.question_text: Which natural language processing (NLP) technique normalizes words before counting them?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Frequency analysis counts word occurrences but does not normalize word forms before counting.

b.explanation: Incorrect. N-grams capture sequences of adjacent words, not word normalization.

c.explanation: Correct. Stemming reduces words to their base or root form (e.g., 'running', 'runs' → 'run') before counting, so variants of the same word are counted together.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Vectorization encodes words as numeric vectors for semantic similarity — it is not primarily used to normalize before counting.

questions.question_text: What is the confidence score returned by the Azure Language in Foundry Tools detection service for an unknown language name?

a.explanation: Incorrect. A score of 1 indicates maximum confidence in a detected language, not an unknown result.

b.explanation: Incorrect. The service does not return -1 for unknown languages.

c.explanation: Correct. When the language cannot be determined, the Azure Language service returns NaN (Not a Number) as the confidence score and 'unknown' as the language name.

d.explanation: Incorrect. 'Unknown' is returned as the language name, but the confidence score for an unknown language is NaN, not the string 'Unknown'.

questions.question_text: What is the first step in the statistical analysis of terms in a text in the context of natural language processing (NLP)?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Vectorization comes after text has been cleaned and tokenized.

b.text: counting the occurrences of each word
b.explanation: Incorrect. Word counting happens after stop words have been removed so that common, uninformative words do not distort frequency analysis.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Encoding into numeric features follows tokenization and stop word removal.

d.explanation: Correct. The first step in statistical text analysis is removing stop words (common words like 'the', 'and', 'is') so that subsequent counting and analysis focuses on meaningful terms.

questions.question_text: Which part of speech synthesis in natural language processing (NLP) involves breaking text into individual words such that each word can be assigned phonetic sounds?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Lemmatization reduces words to their base form for analysis — it is not specific to speech synthesis.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Key phrase extraction identifies important phrases in text — it is not part of the speech synthesis pipeline.

c.explanation: Correct. In speech synthesis, tokenization breaks the text into individual words (tokens) so that each can be converted to phonetic sounds for audio generation.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Transcribing converts audio speech to text (speech-to-text), which is the reverse of synthesis.

questions.question_text: For which two scenarios is the Universal Language Model used by the speech-to-text API optimized? Each correct answer presents a complete solution.

a.explanation: Incorrect. Acoustic modeling is a component of ASR systems, not a scenario the Universal Language Model is optimized for.

b.explanation: Correct. The Universal Language Model is pre-trained and optimized for conversational speech, such as informal dialogues.

c.explanation: Correct. The Universal Language Model is also optimized for dictation — transcribing spoken content such as documents or notes.

d.explanation: Incorrect. 'Language' is too broad; it is not a specific optimization scenario for the Universal Language Model.

e.explanation: Incorrect. Pronunciation assessment is a separate Azure Speech feature, not an optimization scenario for the Universal Language Model.

questions.question_text: Which two types of files can be imported to generate a knowledge base for use with a Microsoft Foundry Agent? Each correct answer presents a complete solution.

a.explanation: Incorrect. CSV files are tabular data files and are not supported as a knowledge base source for Microsoft Foundry Agents.

b.explanation: Correct. Word documents (.doc/.docx) can be imported to generate a knowledge base for a Microsoft Foundry Agent.

c.explanation: Incorrect. MP4 is a video format and is not a supported knowledge base source.

d.explanation: Correct. PDF files are a supported format for importing content to build a knowledge base for a Microsoft Foundry Agent.

e.explanation: Incorrect. ZIP archive files are not a directly supported knowledge base import format.

questions.question_text: Which two Azure AI Services features can be used to enable both text-to-text and speech-to-text between multiple languages? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.

a.explanation: Incorrect. CLU interprets user intent in text — it does not perform translation or speech-to-text conversion.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Key phrase extraction identifies important phrases in text — it does not translate or transcribe speech.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Language detection identifies which language text is written in but does not translate or transcribe across languages.

d.text: the Azure Speech in Foundry Tools
d.explanation: Correct. Azure Speech provides speech-to-text (and text-to-speech) capabilities across multiple languages.

e.explanation: Correct. Azure Translator provides text-to-text translation across multiple languages.

questions.question_text: Which Azure resource provides direct access to both Azure Translator in Foundry Tools and Azure AI Speech services through a single endpoint and authentication key?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Azure AI Bot Service is a platform for building conversational bots — it does not serve as a unified endpoint for Translator and Speech.

b.explanation: Correct. Microsoft Foundry (Azure AI Foundry) provides a unified hub where multiple Azure AI services, including Speech and Translator, are accessible through a single resource endpoint and authentication key.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Machine Learning is for building and training custom ML models — it is not a unified endpoint for Azure AI cognitive services.

d.text: Azure Language in Foundry Tools service
d.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Language provides NLP capabilities specifically — it does not unify Speech and Translator under a single endpoint.

questions.question_text: Which feature of the Azure Translator in Foundry Tools service is available only to Custom Translator?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Document translation (translating entire files) is available in the standard Translator service, not exclusive to Custom Translator.

b.explanation: Correct. Custom Translator allows you to build and train custom translation models using domain-specific bilingual dictionaries, which is not available in the standard Translator service.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Speaker recognition is an Azure Speech feature, not part of Azure Translator at all.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Text translation is the core feature of the standard Translator service and is not exclusive to Custom Translator.

questions.question_text: When using the Azure AI Service for Language, what should you use to provide further information online about entities extracted from a text?

a.explanation: Correct. Entity linking identifies named entities in text and links them to their corresponding entries in an external knowledge base (such as Wikipedia), providing additional online context.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Key phrase extraction identifies the most relevant phrases in text but does not link to external information sources.

c.explanation: Incorrect. NER identifies and classifies entities (people, places, organizations) but does not link them to external knowledge bases.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Text translation converts text between languages — it does not enrich entities with external information.

questions.question_text: Which feature of the Azure Language in Foundry Tools service includes functionality that returns links to external websites to disambiguate terms identified in a text?

a.explanation: Correct. Entity recognition in Azure Language includes entity linking, which returns hyperlinks to external knowledge bases (e.g., Wikipedia) to disambiguate recognized entities.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Key phrase extraction returns the most significant phrases in text — it does not link to external sites.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Language detection identifies the language a text is written in — it does not provide links to external sources.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Sentiment analysis determines the emotional tone of text — it does not link entities to external websites.

questions.question_text: Which three features are elements of the Azure Language in Foundry Tools service? Each correct answer presents a complete solution.

a.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Vision is a separate service for image analysis — it is not a feature of Azure Language.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Content Moderator is a separate Azure AI service — it is not part of the Azure Language service.

c.explanation: Correct. Entity Linking is a feature of Azure Language that links recognized entities to external knowledge bases.

d.explanation: Correct. PII detection is a built-in feature of Azure Language that identifies and can redact sensitive personal information in text.

e.explanation: Correct. Sentiment analysis is a core feature of Azure Language that determines the emotional tone (positive, negative, neutral) of text.

questions.question_text: Which Azure Language in Foundry Tools feature allows you to analyze written articles to extract information and concepts, such as people and locations, for classification purposes?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Content Moderator detects offensive or inappropriate content — it does not extract people or location entities.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Key phrase extraction identifies the most significant phrases in text but does not classify them into entity types like people or locations.

c.explanation: Correct. Named Entity Recognition (NER) identifies and classifies entities in text into categories such as people, organizations, locations, dates, and more.

d.explanation: Incorrect. PII detection focuses specifically on identifying sensitive personal data for privacy protection, not general entity classification.

questions.question_text: What are the two main schema components of a Language Understanding app model? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.

a.explanation: Correct. Entities are the structured data items extracted from an utterance (e.g., a date, location, or product name). They are a core schema component.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Entity linking is a separate Azure Language feature that connects text to external knowledge bases — it is not a schema component of a CLU app.

c.explanation: Correct. Intents represent the purpose or goal behind a user's utterance (e.g., 'BookFlight', 'GetWeather'). They are the other core schema component.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Utterances are example training phrases added to intents — they are training data, not schema components.

questions.question_text: Which three features are elements of the Azure Speech in Foundry Tools service? Each correct answer presents a complete solution.

a.explanation: Incorrect. Document translation is a feature of the Azure Translator service, not Azure Speech.

b.explanation: Correct. Language identification is a feature of Azure Speech that detects which spoken language is present in audio.

c.explanation: Correct. Speaker recognition is a feature of Azure Speech that identifies and verifies speakers by their voice characteristics.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Text translation (converting text between languages) is a feature of Azure Translator, not Azure Speech.

e.explanation: Correct. Voice assistants (custom keyword detection and dialog integration) are supported by Azure Speech as part of its conversational AI capabilities.

questions.question_text: Which natural language processing (NLP) workload is used to generate closed caption text for live presentations?

a.explanation: Correct. Azure Speech provides real-time speech-to-text transcription, which is used to generate closed captions for live audio or video.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Language analyzes text (sentiment, entities, key phrases) — it does not transcribe audio to text.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Translator translates text between languages — it does not transcribe speech.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Text Analytics (now Azure Language) analyzes written text — it does not generate captions from audio.

questions.question_text: Which artificial intelligence (AI) workload scenario is an example of natural language processing (NLP)?

a.text: extracting key phrases from a business insights report
a.explanation: Correct. Key phrase extraction from text is a core NLP task — it analyzes written content to identify the most relevant terms.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Identifying objects in images is a computer vision task (object detection), not NLP.

c.text: monitoring for sudden increases in quantity of failed sign-in attempts
c.explanation: Incorrect. Monitoring sign-in anomalies is an anomaly detection or security analytics task, not NLP.

d.text: predicting whether customers are likely to buy a product based on previous purchases
d.explanation: Incorrect. Predicting purchase likelihood from purchase history is a classification or regression task, not NLP.

questions.question_text: Which two artificial intelligence (AI) workload scenarios are examples of natural language processing (NLP)? Each correct answer presents a complete solution.

a.text: extracting handwritten text from online images
a.explanation: Incorrect. Extracting handwritten text from images is optical character recognition (OCR), which is a computer vision task.

b.text: generating tags and descriptions for images
b.explanation: Incorrect. Generating image tags and descriptions is a computer vision task (image analysis).

c.text: monitoring network traffic for sudden spikes
c.explanation: Incorrect. Monitoring network traffic is anomaly detection — it operates on numeric time-series data, not natural language.

d.text: performing sentiment analysis on social media data
d.explanation: Correct. Sentiment analysis classifies text by emotional tone — this is a core NLP workload.

e.text: translating text between different languages from product reviews
e.explanation: Correct. Translating text between languages is an NLP task performed by services like Azure Translator.

questions.question_text: Which computer vision solution provides the ability to identify a person's age based on a photograph?

a.explanation: Correct. Facial detection (and face analysis) can identify facial attributes including estimated age from a photograph using the Azure Face service.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Image classification assigns an overall label to the whole image — it does not analyze facial attributes like age.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Object detection locates and identifies objects in an image with bounding boxes — it does not perform facial attribute analysis.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Semantic segmentation classifies individual pixels — it does not determine facial attributes.

questions.question_text: Which computer vision service provides bounding box coordinates as part of its output?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Image analysis returns descriptions, tags, and categories for the whole image — not bounding box coordinates for individual objects.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Image classification assigns a single label to the whole image — it does not provide bounding boxes.

c.explanation: Correct. Object detection identifies individual objects within an image and returns bounding box coordinates for the location of each object.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Semantic segmentation classifies each pixel into a category — it does not return bounding box coordinates.

questions.question_text: What allows you to identify different vehicle types in traffic monitoring images?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Image classification assigns a single label to the whole image. It cannot identify and locate multiple different vehicle types within the same image.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Linear regression predicts numeric values — it is not used for visual recognition in images.

c.explanation: Correct. Object detection identifies multiple objects of different types within an image and provides their locations — ideal for identifying different vehicle types in traffic images.

d.explanation: Incorrect. OCR extracts text from images — it does not identify vehicle types.

questions.question_text: What allows you to identify different types of bone fractures in X-ray images?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Conversational AI builds chatbots and dialogue systems — it does not analyze X-ray images.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Facial detection analyzes human faces — it is not applicable to bone fracture analysis.

c.explanation: Correct. Image classification can assign a fracture type label to an X-ray image based on training data with predefined fracture categories.

d.explanation: Incorrect. While object detection could locate fracture regions, classifying fracture types into predefined categories is best described as image classification.

questions.question_text: Which process allows you to use object detection?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Sentiment analysis is an NLP task that processes text — not images.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Extracting text from manuscripts uses OCR, not object detection.

c.text: granting employee access to a secure building
c.explanation: Incorrect. Building access uses facial recognition/verification, not general object detection.

d.explanation: Correct. Object detection identifies and locates objects (livestock) within images or video frames, enabling tracking of individual animals across a field.

questions.question_text: Which artificial intelligence (AI) technique should be used to extract the name of a store from a photograph displaying the store front?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Image classification assigns an overall label to an image — it does not extract text content visible in the image.

b.explanation: Incorrect. NLP processes text that has already been extracted — it does not read text from images.

c.explanation: Correct. OCR reads and extracts printed or handwritten text from images — it can read a store name from a photograph of a storefront sign.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Semantic segmentation classifies each pixel — it does not extract readable text from images.

questions.question_text: What can be used for an attendance system that can scan handwritten signatures?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Face detection identifies human faces in images — it does not read handwritten text or signatures.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Image classification labels the whole image — it does not extract handwritten content.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Object detection locates objects with bounding boxes — it does not read handwritten signatures.

d.explanation: Correct. OCR can read handwritten text from images, making it suitable for scanning handwritten signatures in attendance systems.

questions.question_text: Which feature of computer vision involves associating an image with metadata that summarizes the attributes of the image?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Categorizing places an image into a taxonomy-based content category — it does not attach descriptive attribute metadata tags.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Content organization refers to grouping or indexing images but is not the specific feature that attaches attribute metadata.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Detecting image types identifies whether an image is a clip art, line drawing, or photograph — not summarizing its attributes as metadata tags.

d.explanation: Correct. Tagging associates descriptive metadata tags (attributes like 'outdoor', 'car', 'sunny') with an image, summarizing its visual content.

questions.question_text: Which three parts of the machine learning process does the Azure Vision in Foundry Tools eliminate the need for? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.

a.explanation: Incorrect. You still need to provision an Azure AI resource (endpoint and key) to use Azure Vision in Foundry Tools.

b.explanation: Correct. Azure Vision provides pre-built, pre-selected models — you do not need to choose or design a model architecture.

c.explanation: Correct. Because the models are pre-built and maintained by Microsoft, you do not need to run your own model evaluation.

d.explanation: Incorrect. You still call the API to run inference — that is how you use the service.

e.explanation: Correct. Azure Vision uses pre-trained models — you do not need to collect training data or train a model yourself.

questions.question_text: Which analytical task of the Azure Vision in Foundry Tools service returns bounding box coordinates?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Image categorization assigns a category from a taxonomy to the whole image — it does not return bounding box coordinates.

b.explanation: Correct. Object detection identifies individual objects in an image and returns bounding box coordinates indicating where each object is located.

c.explanation: Incorrect. OCR returns the text content and its position as bounding polygons around text, but bounding boxes for objects are specifically associated with object detection.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Tagging returns descriptive labels for image content — it does not return location coordinates.

questions.question_text: When using the Face Detect API of the Azure Face in Foundry Tools service, which feature helps identify whether a human face has glasses or headwear?

a.explanation: Correct. Face attributes provide information about detected facial characteristics, including accessories such as glasses or headwear.

b.explanation: Incorrect. A face ID is a temporary identifier assigned to a detected face — it does not describe physical attributes.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Face landmarks identify specific facial points (e.g., eye corners, nose tip) — they do not classify accessories.

d.explanation: Incorrect. The face rectangle defines the bounding box around the detected face — it does not provide attribute information.

questions.question_text: When using the Azure Face in Foundry Tools service, what should you use to perform one-to-many or one-to-one face matching? Each correct answer presents a complete solution.

a.explanation: Incorrect. Custom Vision is an image classification/object detection service — it does not perform face matching.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Face attributes describe facial characteristics — they do not perform identity matching.

c.explanation: Correct. Face identification performs one-to-many matching: it compares a detected face against a group of known faces to find the best match.

d.explanation: Correct. Face verification performs one-to-one matching: it determines whether two faces belong to the same person.

e.explanation: Incorrect. 'Find similar faces' finds faces that look alike but is not the primary method for structured one-to-many or one-to-one matching scenarios.

questions.question_text: Which service can you use to train an image classification model?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Vision provides pre-built image analysis — it does not let you train a custom image classification model with your own data.

b.explanation: Correct. Azure AI Custom Vision allows you to upload labeled images and train your own custom image classification or object detection model.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Face is specifically for face detection and recognition — not for training general image classifiers.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Language processes text — it has no image classification capabilities.

questions.question_text: What can you use to analyze images of people to identify whether specific human-related visual features are present?

a.text: Azure AI Face in Foundry Tools
a.explanation: Correct. Azure AI Face detects and analyzes human faces in images, identifying visual features such as facial attributes, landmarks, and accessories.

b.text: Azure AI Video Indexer in Foundry Tools
b.explanation: Incorrect. Video Indexer analyzes video content — while it can detect faces, it is not the primary service for analyzing still images for human visual features.

c.text: Azure Document Intelligence in Foundry Tools
c.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Document Intelligence extracts structured data from forms and documents — it does not analyze human features in images.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Vision provides general image analysis — for human-specific feature analysis such as facial attributes, Azure Face is the appropriate service.

questions.question_text: Which additional piece of information is included with each phrase returned by an image description task of the Azure Vision in Foundry Tools?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Bounding boxes are returned by object detection, not by image description captions.

b.explanation: Correct. Each caption returned by the image description feature includes a confidence score indicating how likely the description accurately represents the image.

c.explanation: Incorrect. The endpoint is a connection detail for the API — it is not included in the image description response payload.

d.explanation: Incorrect. An authentication key is used to call the API — it is not part of the response.

questions.question_text: Which two specialized domain models are supported by Azure Vision in Foundry Tools when categorizing an image? Each correct answer presents a complete solution.

a.explanation: Correct. Azure Vision includes a celebrities domain model that can recognize well-known public figures in images.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Detecting image types (clip art, line drawing, etc.) is a feature of Azure Vision but is not a specialized domain model for categorization.

c.explanation: Correct. Azure Vision includes a landmarks domain model that identifies well-known geographic landmarks and structures in images.

d.explanation: Incorrect. This is not a recognized specialized domain model in Azure Vision.

e.explanation: Incorrect. This is not a recognized specialized domain model in Azure Vision.

questions.question_text: Which two artificial intelligence (AI) workload features are part of the Azure Vision in Foundry Tools service? Each correct answer presents a complete solution.

a.explanation: Incorrect. Entity recognition is a feature of Azure Language (NLP) — it is not part of Azure Vision.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Key phrase extraction is an Azure Language feature — not part of Azure Vision.

c.explanation: Correct. OCR is a core feature of Azure Vision in Foundry Tools, enabling text extraction from images and documents.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Sentiment analysis is an Azure Language feature for analyzing text tone — not part of Azure Vision.

e.explanation: Correct. Spatial analysis is a feature of Azure Vision that analyzes the presence and movement of people in video frames in real time.

questions.question_text: You need a solution that analyzes images to identify objects and scenes, such as vehicles, buildings, and outdoor environments. Which service should you use?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Video Indexer analyzes video content for insights — it is not the primary service for analyzing still images for objects and scenes.

b.text: Azure Document Intelligence in Foundry Tools
b.explanation: Incorrect. Document Intelligence extracts structured data from forms and documents — it does not analyze general scenes and objects.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Face specializes in human face detection and analysis — not general object and scene recognition.

d.explanation: Correct. Azure Vision in Foundry Tools provides image analysis capabilities including object detection, scene recognition, and content tagging for a wide variety of objects and environments.

questions.question_text: Which type of artificial intelligence (AI) workload provides the ability to generate bounding boxes that identify the locations of different types of vehicles in an image?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Image analysis describes the overall content of an image — it does not specifically generate bounding boxes for individual objects.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Image classification assigns a single label to the whole image — it does not locate individual objects with bounding boxes.

c.explanation: Incorrect. OCR extracts text from images — it does not detect or locate vehicle types.

d.explanation: Correct. Object detection identifies multiple objects within an image and returns bounding boxes with coordinates and class labels — ideal for identifying and locating different vehicle types.

questions.question_text: Which type of artificial intelligence (AI) workload has the primary purpose of making large amounts of data searchable?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Image analysis extracts insights from images — its primary purpose is not making data searchable.

b.explanation: Correct. Knowledge mining (Azure AI Search) uses AI to extract insights from large volumes of unstructured data and make it searchable through intelligent indexing.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Object detection identifies and locates objects in images — it is not a search/indexing workload.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Semantic segmentation classifies pixels in images — it does not make data searchable.

questions.question_text: You are exploring solutions to improve the document search and indexing service for employees. You need an AI search solution that will include searching text in various types of documents, such as images. Which type of AI workload is this?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Semantic segmentation classifies image pixels — it is not a document search workload.

b.explanation: Correct. Searching text within images (such as scanned documents or photos) requires OCR, which is a computer vision capability. The AI search solution uses computer vision to extract and index text from images.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Conversational AI handles dialogue and chatbot interactions — it is not a document indexing workload.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Data mining discovers patterns in structured datasets — it is not the same as AI-powered document search that reads text from images.

questions.question_text: Which type of service provides a platform for conversational artificial intelligence (AI)?

a.explanation: Correct. Azure AI Bot Service provides the platform and framework for building, deploying, and managing conversational AI bots across multiple channels.

b.text: Azure Document Intelligence in Foundry Tools
b.explanation: Incorrect. Document Intelligence extracts data from forms and documents — it is not a conversational AI platform.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Vision analyzes images — it is not a conversational AI platform.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Translator translates text between languages — it is not a conversational AI platform.

questions.question_text: Which AI service can be integrated into chat applications and generate content in the form of text?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Language analyzes text (sentiment, entities, key phrases) — it does not generate new text content.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Metrics Advisor monitors time-series data for anomalies — it does not generate conversational text.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Vision analyzes images — it does not generate text for chat applications.

d.explanation: Correct. Azure OpenAI in Foundry Models provides access to large language models (e.g., GPT) that can generate coherent text responses, making it ideal for chat application integration.

questions.question_text: Select the answer that correctly completes the sentence. [Answer choice] can return responses, such as natural language, images, or code, based on natural language input.

a.explanation: Incorrect. Computer vision analyzes images — it does not generate diverse outputs like text or code from natural language prompts.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Deep learning is a technique used by many AI systems — it is not itself the type of AI that specifically returns text, images, or code from natural language input.

c.explanation: Correct. Generative AI models (such as GPT and DALL-E) can produce a wide variety of outputs — natural language, images, code — based on natural language prompts.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Machine learning is a broad field. Traditional ML models predict or classify — they do not generate open-ended natural language, images, or code from prompts.

e.explanation: Incorrect. Reinforcement learning trains agents to take actions in an environment to maximize rewards — it does not generate text, images, or code from natural language input.

questions.question_text: As per the NIST AI Risk Management Framework, what is the first stage to consider when developing a responsible generative AI solution?

a.explanation: Correct. The first stage is to identify the potential harms a generative AI solution could cause before measuring, mitigating, or operating it.

b.text: Measure the presence of potential harms.
b.explanation: Incorrect. Measuring harms is the second stage — you must identify what harms are possible before you can measure them.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Mitigating harms is the third stage — it follows identification and measurement.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Operating the solution is the final stage — after harms have been identified, measured, and mitigated.

questions.question_text: The four-step process to apply responsible AI principles to generative AI includes [Answer choice].

a.text: identifying potential harms, measuring harms, mitigating risks, and planning for deployments
a.explanation: Correct. The four steps are: (1) identify potential harms, (2) measure the presence of harms, (3) mitigate risks, and (4) plan for responsible deployment and operation.

b.text: identifying potential harms, monitoring system performance, retraining models, and deploying updates
b.explanation: Incorrect. Monitoring and retraining are operational concerns, not the structured four-step responsible AI process.

c.text: identifying potential harms, securing infrastructure, encrypting data, and monitoring access
c.explanation: Incorrect. Infrastructure security and access controls are important but are not the responsible AI four-step process for generative AI.

d.text: identifying potential harms, training models, tuning hyperparameters, and validating accuracy
d.explanation: Incorrect. Model training and tuning are ML engineering steps, not the responsible AI framework steps.

questions.question_text: Generative AI is used to generate [Answer choice].

a.text: classifications that assign data to predefined categories
a.explanation: Incorrect. Assigning data to predefined categories describes classification, not generative AI.

b.text: new content based on patterns learned from training data
b.explanation: Correct. Generative AI models learn patterns from training data and use those patterns to generate novel content — text, images, code, audio, and more.

c.text: new datasets created during the model training process
c.explanation: Incorrect. Generative AI generates output content from prompts — it does not produce new training datasets as its purpose.

d.text: predictions about future values in structured datasets
d.explanation: Incorrect. Predicting future values from structured data describes regression or time-series forecasting, not generative AI.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes a capability of a generative AI model?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Classifying into predefined categories describes a classification model, not a generative model.

b.text: detects patterns in data to predict future outcomes
b.explanation: Incorrect. Detecting patterns to predict outcomes describes predictive ML (regression, forecasting) — not generative AI.

c.text: generates new content based on learned patterns
c.explanation: Correct. Generative AI models learn patterns from training data and use them to produce new, original content such as text, images, or code.

d.text: uses rule-based logic to automate decisions
d.explanation: Incorrect. Rule-based systems follow explicitly programmed logic — generative AI learns patterns from data rather than following predefined rules.

questions.question_text: A key feature of a generative AI model is that it can [Answer choice].

a.text: assign labels to data by using classification logic
a.explanation: Incorrect. Label assignment using classification logic describes a discriminative model, not a generative one.

b.text: follow predefined rules to make deterministic decisions
b.explanation: Incorrect. Predefined rule-based systems are not AI models — generative models learn from data.

c.text: identify anomalies in large volumes of structured data
c.explanation: Incorrect. Anomaly detection is a specific ML task — not the key feature of generative AI.

d.text: produce original outputs based on patterns learned from training data
d.explanation: Correct. The defining feature of generative AI is its ability to produce novel, original outputs (text, images, code) by learning and applying patterns from training data.

questions.question_text: What are two capabilities of generative AI models? Each correct answer presents a complete solution.

a.text: classifying data into predefined categories, such as spam and non-spam email messages
a.explanation: Incorrect. Email spam classification is a discriminative classification task, not a generative AI capability.

b.text: detecting patterns in structured datasets for predictive analysis
b.explanation: Incorrect. Detecting patterns for prediction describes traditional ML regression or classification, not generative AI.

c.text: generating new content based on training data
c.explanation: Correct. Generating novel content (text, images, code) from learned patterns is the core capability of generative AI.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Object identification in images is a computer vision task (object detection), not generative AI.

e.explanation: Correct. Summarizing text and translating between languages are both generative AI capabilities, producing new text output based on input.

questions.question_text: You need to identify which scenarios benefit from using generative AI. Which two scenarios are common use cases for generative AI? Each correct answer presents a complete solution.

a.explanation: Incorrect. Anomaly detection identifies unusual patterns in data — this is a traditional ML task, not a primary generative AI use case.

b.explanation: Correct. Generating images from text prompts (e.g., with DALL-E) is a core generative AI use case.

c.explanation: Correct. Summarizing long documents or conversations into concise text is a common generative AI use case.

d.explanation: Incorrect. While LLMs can translate, dedicated translation services (Azure Translator) handle this — translation is not listed as a primary generative AI use case in the AI-900 context.

questions.question_text: Which two capabilities are examples of a GPT model? Each correct answer presents a complete solution.

a.explanation: Correct. GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) models can generate coherent, contextually relevant natural language text.

b.text: Detect specific dialects of a language.
b.explanation: Incorrect. Dialect detection is a specialized linguistics task — it is not a primary capability of GPT models.

c.text: Generate closed captions in real-time from a video.
c.explanation: Incorrect. Real-time captioning from video audio is a function of Azure Speech (speech-to-text), not GPT models.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Speech synthesis (text-to-speech) is performed by Azure Speech — not by GPT language models.

e.explanation: Correct. GPT models can understand the meaning and intent of natural language input, enabling them to respond appropriately.

questions.question_text: Which three capabilities are examples of image generation features for a generative AI model? Each correct answer presents a complete solution.

a.explanation: Incorrect. Animating static images is a video/motion graphics capability — it is not a standard image generation feature of generative AI models like DALL-E.

b.explanation: Correct. Generative AI image models can produce multiple variations of an existing image with different styles or details.

c.explanation: Correct. Image generation models support inpainting and editing — modifying specific parts of an existing image based on prompts.

d.text: extracting RGB values from an image
d.explanation: Incorrect. Extracting RGB values is a basic image processing operation, not a generative AI capability.

e.explanation: Correct. Creating entirely new images from natural language text prompts is the primary image generation capability of models like DALL-E.

questions.question_text: Which generative AI model is used to generate images based on natural language prompts?

a.explanation: Correct. DALL-E is a generative AI model developed by OpenAI (available in Azure OpenAI) that creates images from natural language text descriptions.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Embeddings models represent text as numeric vectors for similarity search — they do not generate images.

c.explanation: Incorrect. GPT-3.5 is a text generation model — it generates natural language, not images.

d.explanation: Incorrect. GPT-4 is a multimodal language model focused on text generation and understanding — image generation is the role of DALL-E.

e.explanation: Incorrect. Whisper is a speech recognition model that transcribes audio to text — it does not generate images.

questions.question_text: Select the answer that correctly completes the sentence. [Answer choice] can search, classify, and compare sources of text for similarity.

a.explanation: Incorrect. Data grounding connects AI models to real-world or up-to-date information — it does not itself search or classify text for similarity.

b.explanation: Correct. Embeddings convert text into numeric vectors that capture semantic meaning, enabling similarity search, classification, and comparison of text sources.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Machine learning is a broad field. Embeddings specifically are the technique used for text similarity tasks.

d.explanation: Incorrect. System messages provide instructions to an AI model at the start of a conversation — they do not search or classify text.

questions.question_text: The Microsoft Foundry models catalog is a centralized hub for [Answer choice].

a.text: discovering and comparing generative AI models available in Azure
a.explanation: Correct. The Microsoft Foundry models catalog is a centralized hub where developers can browse, compare, and evaluate available generative AI models from Microsoft and third-party providers.

b.text: fine-tuning and deploying models by using Microsoft Foundry
b.explanation: Incorrect. Fine-tuning and deployment are actions you can take on models — the catalog's primary role is discovery and comparison.

c.text: monitoring model usage and performance in generative AI applications
c.explanation: Incorrect. Monitoring is an operational concern separate from the catalog, which focuses on model discovery.

d.text: testing model prompts and coauthoring prompt flows with team members
d.explanation: Incorrect. Prompt testing and collaborative prompt flow development are features of Microsoft Foundry's playground and prompt flow tools, not the models catalog.

questions.question_text: The Microsoft Foundry models catalog can be used to [Answer choice].

a.explanation: Correct. The models catalog allows you to browse and compare available generative AI models by provider, capability, and benchmarks.

b.explanation: Incorrect. The catalog is for discovering and deploying existing models — not for creating new models from scratch.

c.explanation: Incorrect. While you can initiate deployment from the catalog, the catalog's primary purpose is discovery and comparison, not deployment configuration.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Training custom models requires Azure Machine Learning or fine-tuning tools — the catalog is for discovering pre-built models.

questions.question_text: Which statement accurately describes Microsoft Foundry?

a.text: Microsoft Foundry is a centralized hub for discovering and comparing generative AI models available in Azure.
a.explanation: Incorrect. This describes the models catalog within Microsoft Foundry — the overall platform is broader than just model discovery.

b.text: Microsoft Foundry is a centralized platform that brings together tools and services for building, testing, and deploying generative AI solutions.
b.explanation: Correct. Microsoft Foundry (Azure AI Foundry) is a unified development platform that integrates tools, models, and Azure AI services to help developers build end-to-end generative AI applications.

c.text: Microsoft Foundry is a marketplace that allows developers to purchase from and sell generative AI models from multiple providers.
c.explanation: Incorrect. Microsoft Foundry is not a commercial marketplace for buying and selling AI models.

d.text: Microsoft Foundry is a platform that enables developers to directly customize and train large language models (LLMs) from scratch.
d.explanation: Incorrect. Microsoft Foundry focuses on building with and fine-tuning existing models — training LLMs from scratch requires a different scale of infrastructure.

questions.question_text: What does the Microsoft Foundry models catalog provide?

a.text: a catalog of generative AI models that you can discover and compare
a.explanation: Correct. The models catalog provides a searchable, filterable catalog of generative AI models — including Azure OpenAI models and third-party models — that developers can explore and compare.

b.text: a catalog of pretrained datasets used to train generative AI models
b.explanation: Incorrect. The catalog lists models, not the datasets used to train them.

c.text: a model management service for monitoring and tuning deployed models
c.explanation: Incorrect. Model monitoring and tuning are operational tasks handled by other tools — the catalog is for discovery and comparison.

d.text: a set of prebuilt generative AI apps and sample solutions
d.explanation: Incorrect. The catalog lists AI models — not pre-assembled applications or solution templates.

questions.question_text: Microsoft Foundry can be used to [Answer choice].

a.text: build, customize, and manage AI apps and agents
a.explanation: Correct. Microsoft Foundry provides the tools to develop, customize, evaluate, and deploy AI applications and intelligent agents end-to-end.

b.text: monitor the performance metrics of virtual machines
b.explanation: Incorrect. VM performance monitoring is handled by Azure Monitor — not Microsoft Foundry, which focuses on AI application development.

c.text: store and version structured training data
c.explanation: Incorrect. Training data versioning is managed through Azure Machine Learning datasets or Azure storage solutions — not a core function of Microsoft Foundry.

d.text: train large language models (LLMs) from raw datasets
d.explanation: Incorrect. Training LLMs from scratch requires massive infrastructure — Microsoft Foundry is for building with and fine-tuning existing models, not pre-training new LLMs.

questions.question_text: Azure OpenAI in Foundry Models can be used to [Answer choice].

a.text: access and deploy models, such as GPT, for generative AI solutions
a.explanation: Correct. Azure OpenAI in Foundry Models provides access to OpenAI's generative models (GPT-4, GPT-3.5, DALL-E, etc.) within Azure, with enterprise security and compliance.

b.text: build and train computer vision models from labeled images
b.explanation: Incorrect. Training custom computer vision models from labeled images is done with Azure AI Custom Vision, not Azure OpenAI.

c.text: manage API keys and secrets used by Azure AI services
c.explanation: Incorrect. API key and secret management is handled by Azure Key Vault — not a function of Azure OpenAI.

d.text: orchestrate model training pipelines for custom machine learning workflows
d.explanation: Incorrect. ML pipeline orchestration is a function of Azure Machine Learning — not Azure OpenAI, which is focused on inference with pre-trained generative models.

questions.question_text: You plan to use Azure OpenAI in Microsoft Foundry for a generative AI solution. Which security capability is supported by using Azure OpenAI in Foundry Models?

a.explanation: Correct. Azure OpenAI in Foundry Models supports Azure RBAC, allowing administrators to control who can access and manage the resource using Azure's identity and access management framework.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Azure OpenAI uses server-side encryption managed by Azure — client-side encryption keys are not a supported security capability.

c.explanation: Incorrect. While network restrictions can be configured, 'custom firewall rules' is not the specific supported security capability associated with Azure OpenAI in Foundry Models.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Azure OpenAI is a managed cloud service — users do not have access to the underlying operating system to configure permissions.

questions.question_text: You plan to build a generative AI chatbot that will provide medical information. You need to select an Azure service that ensures access to the latest generative AI models, along with built-in safety systems and responsible AI controls. Which service should you use?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Language provides NLP analysis capabilities — it does not provide access to generative models with built-in safety and responsible AI controls.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Machine Learning is a platform for training and deploying custom ML models — it does not specifically provide access to the latest generative AI models with built-in safety systems.

c.explanation: Correct. Azure OpenAI in Foundry Models provides access to the latest OpenAI generative models with Azure's enterprise safety features, content filtering, and responsible AI controls — making it appropriate for a medical chatbot.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Azure Vision analyzes images — it does not provide generative language models for chatbot development.

questions.question_text: For which audience is Microsoft Foundry primarily intended?

a.text: AI developers and technical decision-makers that build generative AI apps and custom copilots
a.explanation: Correct. Microsoft Foundry is designed for developers and technical architects who need a unified platform to build, evaluate, and deploy generative AI applications and intelligent agents.

b.text: business users who want to build machine learning models without writing code
b.explanation: Incorrect. No-code ML model building for business users is more aligned with Azure ML's AutoML or Power BI — not Microsoft Foundry, which targets developers.

c.text: data scientists focused on training and managing large-scale custom machine learning models
c.explanation: Incorrect. Data scientists training large-scale custom ML models primarily use Azure Machine Learning — Microsoft Foundry focuses on generative AI application development.

d.text: Windows application developers that integrate pretrained machine learning models
d.explanation: Incorrect. Windows application developers integrating ML models would use Windows ML or ONNX runtime — not Microsoft Foundry, which is cloud-based and generative AI focused.

questions.question_text: Which principle of responsible artificial intelligence (AI) involves evaluating and mitigating the bias introduced by the features of a model?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Accountability defines governance structures and organizational responsibility for AI decisions — not the active process of detecting and reducing bias in model features.

b.explanation: Correct. Fairness requires that AI systems treat all people equitably. This includes evaluating model features for bias and mitigating any disparate impact on different groups.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Privacy focuses on protecting personal data — not on feature-level bias in models.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Transparency is about making AI systems and their decisions understandable — not specifically about feature bias evaluation.

questions.question_text: Which principle of responsible artificial intelligence (AI) defines the framework of governance and organization principles that meet ethical and legal standards of AI solutions?

a.explanation: Correct. Accountability establishes that people and organizations are responsible for AI systems and their impacts, requiring governance frameworks that meet ethical and legal standards.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Fairness focuses on equitable treatment across demographic groups — it does not define governance frameworks.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Inclusiveness ensures AI empowers all people and does not exclude groups — it is not primarily about governance frameworks.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Transparency focuses on explainability and openness of AI decisions — not the governance framework structure.

questions.question_text: Which principle of responsible artificial intelligence (AI) ensures that an AI system meets any legal and ethical standards it must abide by?

a.explanation: Correct. Accountability requires that AI systems have processes and oversight to ensure compliance with applicable legal and ethical standards.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Fairness is about equitable treatment — while related to ethics, it does not specifically encompass legal compliance frameworks.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Inclusiveness focuses on ensuring AI benefits and includes all people — it is not specifically about legal and ethical compliance.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Privacy and security protect personal data and prevent breaches — they are important but do not encompass the full scope of legal and ethical standards compliance.

questions.question_text: Which principle of responsible artificial intelligence (AI) plays the primary role when implementing an AI solution that evaluates qualifications for business loan approvals?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Accountability is important, but when an AI makes decisions that could disadvantage certain groups (e.g., loan denials by demographic), fairness is the primary concern.

b.explanation: Correct. Loan approval AI systems can encode historical biases that disadvantage protected groups. Fairness is the primary principle — the system must evaluate all applicants equitably regardless of demographic characteristics.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Inclusiveness focuses on designing AI that benefits all people — fairness is more specifically about equal treatment in consequential decisions like loan approvals.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Safety relates to reliability and preventing physical harm — fairness is the primary concern for financial decision-making AI.

questions.question_text: Which principle of responsible artificial intelligence (AI) is applied in the design of an AI system to ensure that users understand the constraints and limitations of AI?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Fairness addresses equitable treatment — it does not specifically address communicating system limitations to users.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Inclusiveness focuses on designing AI that benefits all people — it does not specifically address explaining system limitations.

c.explanation: Incorrect. Privacy and security protect personal data — they are not about communicating AI system limitations.

d.explanation: Correct. Transparency requires that AI systems and their limitations be understandable to the people who use them, enabling informed decisions and appropriate trust in AI outputs.

questions.question_text: Which two principles of responsible artificial intelligence (AI) are most important when designing an AI system to manage healthcare data? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.

a.explanation: Correct. Healthcare AI systems have life-affecting consequences — accountability ensures clear governance, oversight, and responsibility for decisions made by or with AI.

b.explanation: Incorrect. While fairness is always relevant, the most critical concerns for healthcare data management are protecting sensitive personal information (privacy) and ensuring responsible oversight (accountability).

c.explanation: Incorrect. Inclusiveness is important broadly, but it is not the primary concern when managing sensitive healthcare data.

d.explanation: Correct. Healthcare data contains highly sensitive personal information. Privacy and security are paramount — protecting patient data from unauthorized access and misuse is a legal and ethical requirement.

questions.question_text: A company is currently developing driverless agriculture vehicles to help harvest crops. The vehicles will be deployed alongside people working in the crop fields. Which principle of responsible artificial intelligence (AI) is most important in this case?

a.explanation: Incorrect. Accountability is important, but when autonomous machines operate near people, ensuring they function correctly and safely is the primary concern.

b.explanation: Incorrect. Inclusiveness focuses on ensuring AI benefits all people — it is not the primary concern for autonomous vehicles operating in close proximity to workers.

c.explanation: Correct. Autonomous vehicles operating alongside people pose direct physical risks. Reliability and safety — ensuring the system works as intended and does not cause harm — is the most important principle in this case.

d.explanation: Incorrect. Transparency is about explainability — while useful, it is secondary to ensuring the vehicles are safe and reliable when deployed near workers.
